content:
First note that since all the \(a_i\) lie strictly between 2 and 3, any integer approximation \(A_i\) must be either 2 or 3.  Writing
\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^7a_i=19,
\]
we need
\[
\sum_{i=1}^7A_i=19,
\]
and we wish to minimize 
\[
M=\max_{1\le i\le7}|A_i-a_i|.
\]
If we let \(k\) of the \(A_i\)ʼs be 3 and the other \(7-k\) be 2, then
\[
3k+2(7-k)=19\quad\Longrightarrow\quad14+k=19\quad\Longrightarrow\quad k=5.
\]
Hence exactly five of the \(A_i\)ʼs must be 3, and two of them must be 2.  Define for each \(i\)

 • \(d_i^{\rm low}=a_i-2\)  (error if we choose \(A_i=2\)),  
 • \(d_i^{\rm high}=3-a_i\)  (error if we choose \(A_i=3\)).  

Numerically,

\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccc}
i      &1    &2    &3    &4    &5    &6    &7\\\hline
a_i    &2.56 &2.61 &2.65 &2.71 &2.79 &2.82 &2.86\\
d_i^{\rm low}&0.56 &0.61 &0.65 &0.71 &0.79 &0.82 &0.86\\
d_i^{\rm high}&0.44&0.39&0.35&0.29&0.21&0.18&0.14
\end{array}
\]
To keep the maximum error small, one floors (takes \(A_i=2\)) on the two smallest \(d_i^{\rm low}\), namely \(i=1,2\), and ceilings (takes \(A_i=3\)) on the other five.  Then

 – the worst‐case error among the “2”’s is \(\max\{0.56,0.61\}=0.61\),  
 – the worst‐case error among the “3”’s is \(\max\{0.35,0.29,0.21,0.18,0.14\}=0.35\).

Thus
\[
M=\max(0.61,\,0.35)=0.61,
\]
and hence \(100M=61\).  Therefore the answer is

\(\boxed{61}\).
